title: When you don't know what to do next
prompt:
I'm working on [PROJECT NAME]. Here's where I am right now: [DESCRIBE CURRENT STATE IN YOUR OWN WORDS, EVEN IF MESSY].

I feel stuck because [WHAT'S BLOCKING YOU].

Before suggesting next steps, ask me 3 clarifying questions that will help you give better advice. Don't skip this step even if my message feels clear.
whyItWorks: Forcing Claude to ask questions first prevents generic advice. The best sessions start with Claude understanding your real situation, not its assumption of it.

title: When an error message doesn't make sense
prompt:
I got this error and I don't understand what it means or how to fix it:

[PASTE ENTIRE ERROR, INCLUDING ANY STACK TRACE]

I'm not a developer. Please:
1. Tell me in plain English what this error is actually saying
2. Tell me the most likely cause based on the project context
3. Give me the specific fix as a step-by-step with exact commands
4. Tell me how I'd avoid this next time
whyItWorks: The explicit non-developer framing changes Claude's default response shape. The numbered structure prevents it from jumping straight to a fix you can't apply.

title: Starting a new Claude Code project from scratch
prompt:
Create a monorepo project called [PROJECT-NAME] with this structure:

/frontend - [YOUR STACK, e.g. React + Vite + Tailwind]
/backend - [YOUR STACK, e.g. Python FastAPI + PostgreSQL]
/.github/workflows - GitHub Actions (empty for now)

Root files:
- README.md explaining what this project is
- .gitignore covering node_modules, .env, __pycache__, .DS_Store, dist, venv
- CLAUDE.md that will be the persistent context for future Claude Code sessions

In /frontend, initialize the project with [STACK] and install: [PACKAGES]

In /backend, create:
- requirements.txt with [DEPENDENCIES]
- .env.example with placeholder variables
- app/main.py with a /health endpoint

Print the full folder tree when done.
whyItWorks: Explicit scaffolding beats 'build me an app' every time. Claude Code does its best work when it knows exactly what to create and where.

title: The CLAUDE.md file that makes every future session better
prompt:
Create a CLAUDE.md at the project root. This file is read at the start of every Claude Code session. Include these sections:

## Project
[One-sentence description of what this app is and who it's for.]

## Tech Stack
[List the frameworks, databases, services used, one per line.]

## Conventions
- File naming: [your pattern]
- Component structure: [your pattern]
- State management: [your approach]

## Known Gotchas
[Things you've already hit and fixed. Example: 'Supabase auth requires anon key in browser, service role only on server.']

## What NOT to do
[Approaches you've already rejected. Example: 'Don't suggest Redux, we're using Zustand.']

## Current Focus
[What you're working on right now, updated as it changes.]
whyItWorks: This is the single highest-leverage file in a Claude Code project. Most people skip it. Those who use it get dramatically better output in every session.

title: Breaking a big feature into 5-minute tasks
prompt:
I want to build [FEATURE]. Before you start coding anything:

1. Review the existing codebase and summarize what's relevant to this feature
2. Break the work into tasks that would each take no more than 5 minutes
3. Show me the full list and wait for my approval before starting
4. Execute one task at a time, showing me the result after each
5. If you hit something unexpected, stop and ask rather than guessing

Do not touch any files outside what's necessary for this feature.
whyItWorks: Claude Code wanders when given too much autonomy. Forcing 5-minute chunks with check-ins keeps you in control and surfaces problems before they compound.

title: Getting Claude to act like a domain expert, not just a coder
prompt:
You are both an AI app developer AND a world-leading expert in [DOMAIN, e.g., crested gecko genetics / credit repair law / mushroom identification].

While you build this feature, apply domain knowledge to every design decision. Don't build generic scaffolding. Specifically:

- Use terminology a [DOMAIN EXPERT] would actually use
- Anticipate edge cases someone in this field would know about
- Structure the data model based on how real [DOMAIN USERS] actually think about the information

Reference these source materials as authoritative: [LINK TO YOUR DOCS, URLS, OR UPLOADED FILES]
whyItWorks: Claude has deep knowledge of most fields. Telling it to wear that hat while coding produces work that feels native to your industry instead of like a generic template.

title: Making Claude ask better questions instead of assuming
prompt:
Before you answer my next request, I want you to operate under this rule for our entire conversation:

When I give you a task, if there's any ambiguity, ask me up to 3 clarifying questions before you start. Use the ask_user_input tool if it's available so I can click answers instead of typing.

Don't ask permission to ask. Just ask.
whyItWorks: This single instruction transforms the quality of every subsequent interaction. Claude's default is to guess and proceed. Flipping that default to 'ask first' catches scope mistakes before you've wasted time.

title: Getting Claude to disagree with you when you're wrong
prompt:
For the rest of this conversation, I want you to push back on my ideas when you see problems with them, rather than defaulting to encouragement.

If I'm about to make a scope mistake, tell me. If I'm underestimating complexity, say so. If my plan has a hole, name it.

Stay kind but direct. Treat me like a peer who can handle honest feedback, not a customer you're trying to please.
whyItWorks: Claude's defaults lean toward agreeable and helpful. This is fine for most things, but terrible when you need a real sounding board. This instruction unlocks the useful-critic mode.

title: Deep research that doesn't waste your time
prompt:
I need you to go deep on [TOPIC]. This is for [PURPOSE, e.g., launching a product / making a decision / learning the basics].

Please:
1. Search multiple angles of this topic, not just one
2. Cite sources as you go, especially for anything statistical or recent
3. Flag where expert consensus is strong vs. where it's contested
4. Identify what an insider would know that an outsider wouldn't
5. End with 'what I'd do if this were my problem' - your actual recommendation

I'm going to be away for [TIME]. Go as deep as the topic warrants. Don't rush.
whyItWorks: Setting a long time budget and giving Claude explicit permission to go deep produces reports 10x better than quick queries. Most people underuse this mode.

title: Understanding a technical concept without pretending to be technical
prompt:
Explain [CONCEPT] to me. I have no coding background. I've heard the term used but I don't have a real grip on what it means.

Please:
1. Start with a one-sentence plain-English definition
2. Give me a real-world analogy that's not cringe
3. Show me when this concept actually matters in practice for someone building apps with AI
4. Tell me what I can do without understanding this deeply, versus what I'd need to actually learn
5. If I were going to explain this to someone else later, what's the one sentence I'd use?
whyItWorks: This structure produces explanations you actually retain. The 'explain it to someone else' step tests whether you understood it.

title: Writing landing page copy that doesn't sound like AI wrote it
prompt:
I'm writing a landing page for [PRODUCT]. Before you draft anything:

1. Ask me who specifically this is for - not a demographic, a real person
2. Ask me what they're feeling the moment they land on this page
3. Ask me what they need to believe to buy

Then write the copy in my voice, which is [DESCRIBE OR LINK TO EXAMPLES]. Avoid: em dashes, 'unlock your potential,' 'transform your life,' 'harness the power of,' 'in today's fast-paced world.'

The copy should sound like one human talking to another specific human, not a brand talking to an audience.
whyItWorks: The three questions at the top force you to articulate positioning you probably haven't written down. The explicit banned phrases catch the AI tells that kill conversion.

title: Generating viral-hook-style content from a single insight
prompt:
I have an insight I want to turn into [NUMBER] short-form posts (Twitter/LinkedIn/TikTok script).

The insight is: [YOUR INSIGHT IN ONE PARAGRAPH]

For each post:
- Lead with a hook that makes someone stop scrolling
- Deliver the insight in a way that feels earned, not lectured
- End with something that makes them think or act

Show me 10 variations. Vary the tone: some confident, some vulnerable, some contrarian, some informational. I'll pick the ones that feel most like me.
whyItWorks: Asking for 10 variations with varied tone beats asking for the 'best' version. You pick what fits your voice and discover patterns you couldn't have specified up front.

title: Finding the one sentence that sells your product
prompt:
I'm trying to find the one sentence that captures what my product is and who it's for.

Here's what the product does: [DESCRIBE FUNCTIONALLY]
Here's who it's for: [SPECIFIC BUYER]
Here's what they feel before they buy: [EMOTIONAL STATE]
Here's what they get out of it: [OUTCOME]

Generate 20 candidate sentences. Each should:
- Be under 20 words
- Name the buyer specifically (even if subtly)
- Promise a specific outcome, not a vague feeling
- Avoid jargon that would only land with insiders

After the list, pick your top 3 and explain why each would convert a cold visitor.
whyItWorks: Twenty options forces Claude past its default formulations. The 'top 3 with reasoning' step teaches you what's actually working in the good ones.

title: Setting up Supabase for a new project
prompt:
I'm setting up Supabase for a new project. Walk me through it assuming I've never done this before. I need:

1. How to create the project in the Supabase dashboard
2. What schema to create for [BRIEFLY DESCRIBE YOUR DATA - users, items, relationships]
3. How to set up Row Level Security policies so users can only see their own data
4. How to connect my [FRAMEWORK] app using environment variables
5. The exact commands I'd run to test the connection works

Go slow. Assume I'll get confused at UI changes and give me screenshots-description-level detail.
whyItWorks: Infrastructure setup is where non-coders bail out. This prompt fights that by demanding walkthrough-level detail up front.

title: Figuring out what to charge
prompt:
Help me figure out what to charge for [PRODUCT].

Here's what it does: [DESCRIPTION]
Here's who it's for: [BUYER]
Here's what similar products charge: [IF YOU KNOW]
Here's what I'd like to charge: [IF YOU HAVE AN INSTINCT]

Walk me through:
1. What pricing models make sense here (one-time, subscription, freemium, tiered)
2. What the market will bear for this buyer type
3. Where my instinct might be wrong (too low? too high?)
4. A specific price and model you'd recommend, with reasoning
5. How I'd know if I got it wrong in the first month

Be direct. Don't hedge.
whyItWorks: Pricing is where solo builders self-sabotage most. Forcing Claude to recommend a specific number with reasoning cuts through your own anxiety.
